content:
First, label the trapezoid so that \(AD\parallel BC\), \(BC=b\), \(AD=a\), and the equal legs \(AB=CD=\ell\).  Its perimeter is
\[
2\ell +a+b=120,
\]
and in \(\triangle ABC\) the sides are \(\ell,b,d\) where \(d=AC\).  Its perimeter is
\[
\ell +b +d=114.
\]
In an isosceles trapezoid the diagonals are equal, and one checks
\[
\ell^2=\Bigl(\frac{b-a}2\Bigr)^2+h^2,\qquad
d^2=\Bigl(\frac{b+a}2\Bigr)^2+h^2,
\]
so
\[
d^2-\ell^2=ab.
\]
If \(I_1,I_2\) are the incenters of \(\triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle DBC\), one finds by the usual “incenter‐as a length‐weighted average of the vertices” that
\[
I_2-I_1
=\frac1{114}\Bigl[b\,(D-A)+(d-\ell)\,(C-B)\Bigr],
\]
and in our convenient coordinates this becomes a purely horizontal vector of length
\[
\frac{b\,(a+d-\ell)}{114}=16,
\]
i.e.\ 
\[
b\,(a+d-\ell)=1824.
\]
Hence the unknowns \(a,b,\ell,d\) satisfy
\[
\begin{cases}
2\ell+a+b=120,\\
\ell+b+d=114,\\
d^2-\ell^2=ab,\\
b\,(a+d-\ell)=1824.
\end{cases}
\]
One solves in order to get
\[
(a,b,\ell,d)=(22,48,25,41).
\]
Then from 
\[
\ell^2=\Bigl(\tfrac{b-a}2\Bigr)^2 +h^2
\]
one finds 
\[
h^2=25^2-13^2=625-169=456,
\]
so the height \(h=\sqrt{456}=2\sqrt{114}\).  Hence the area of the trapezoid is
\[
\frac{a+b}2\;h
=\frac{22+48}2\;2\sqrt{114}
=35\cdot 2\sqrt{114}
=70\sqrt{114}.
\]
Thus \(m=70\), \(n=114\), and
\[
100m+n=100\cdot70+114=7000+114=\boxed{7114}.
\]
